one_line_takeaway: A descriptive audit of 36 small open-weight LLMs finds 56% of keylogger-code injection attempts succeed and an 'instruction sandwich' prompt is most effective, but its headline 'parameters drive vulnerability' claim is undercut by its own regression/clustering showing larger models were slightly more resistant.
key_finding: Across the 144 trials, 56% of tests succeeded. Per-prompt vulnerability rose from P1/P2 (53% each) to P3 (58%) to P4 (61%), i.e., the 'instruction sandwich' was the most effective. 28% of models (10/36) failed all four prompts and only 14% (5/36) resisted all four. P1 and P2 successes are strongly correlated (Pearson 0.71) while P2-P4 are weakly correlated (0.28), suggesting some prompts exploit shared and others distinct weaknesses. Feature-importance analyses rank parameter count above model type (random forest 0.75 vs 0.25; SHAP 0.147 vs 0.075), but the DIRECTION is the opposite of the abstract's 'parameters correlate with vulnerability' framing: logistic regression gives a negative parameter coefficient (-0.34), and the PCA cluster with the largest models (Cluster 1) has the LOWEST vulnerability while the smallest-parameter cluster (Cluster 2) is most vulnerable-so within this 0.5b-14b range, larger models were slightly MORE resistant.
core_contribution: An empirical, descriptive study of how susceptible 36 small open-weight LLMs are to direct prompt-injection attacks whose specific goal is to coax the model into producing usable/adaptable Python keylogger code. The authors craft four attack prompts (P1-P4), each exploiting a different social-engineering framing (troubleshooting pretext, curiosity/movie framing, educational-purpose claim, and an 'instruction sandwich' hiding the malicious request between benign ones), run them against all 36 models via Cloudflare Workers AI's LLM Playground, and produce a 36x4 success table that they then subject to descriptive statistics plus correlation, random-forest feature importance, SHAP, logistic regression, and PCA/K-means clustering to relate vulnerability to model parameter count and model type. The contribution is the measurement and the parameter-vs-type analysis for a single narrow attack objective, NOT a new attack technique or defense.
threat_model: Direct prompt injection (jailbreaking): an adversary interacts directly with a deployed LLM and uses deceptive framing to bypass safety guardrails and elicit content the model should refuse-here, functional Python keylogger code that could capture keystrokes/credentials. The paper motivates this with OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (prompt injection ranked #1) and MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051, and ties keyloggers to real APT usage (APT28, APT41, Sandworm; 2015 Ukraine grid attack) via MITRE ATT&CK T1056.001. The attacker capability assumed is single-turn text-only input with no system access; no indirect (data-source-embedded) injection is actually tested despite being defined in the background.
limitations: (1) Very narrow scope: a single attack objective (keylogger code) and only four prompts, which the authors themselves flag as a limitation; findings may not generalize to other injection goals. (2) The model set is all small open-weight models (0.5b-14b parameters) hosted on one platform-no frontier/closed models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) are tested, so parameter-count conclusions are extrapolated from a narrow, low range. (3) Ground truth is a single human judge with no inter-rater reliability or rubric, making the 56% figure subjective and non-reproducible. (4) Statistical machinery (random forest, SHAP, PCA, logistic regression) is applied to just 36 points with 2 features-severely underpowered and prone to overfitting; the reported 86% logistic-regression accuracy on n=36 is not validated out-of-sample. (5) Internal inconsistency: the abstract/framing claim parameters drive vulnerability, yet the regression and clustering show larger models were slightly less vulnerable-feature importance measures magnitude, not direction, and the paper conflates the two. (6) The rich high-success numbers in the Background (97.2%, 98.3%, 100%) are prior related work, not this study's results. (7) Only direct injection is tested despite indirect injection being defined; no defenses are empirically evaluated (mitigations are only recommended from OWASP/MITRE).

datasets_used: Authors' own 36x4 prompt-injection outcome table (144 trials): 4 crafted prompts P1-P4 tested against 36 LLMs on Cloudflare Workers AI LLM Playground, October 2024

36 open-weight models spanning 0.5b-14b parameters (e.g., qwen1.5-0.5b-chat, tinyllama-1.1b, llama-3.2-1b/3b-instruct, mistral-7b-instruct variants, llama-2-7b/13b-chat, qwen1.5-14b-chat, deepseek-coder-6.7b, deepseek-math-7b, llama-3.2-11b-vision-instruct, llamaguard-7b) categorized as Chat, Instruct, Code-Specific, Domain-Specific, Multimodal, or Other (Appendix A)
No external benchmark dataset; the initial exploratory pool of 50 injection prompts was internally generated
quantitative_results: Overall attack success: 56% of 144 trials succeeded, 44% resisted
Per-prompt vulnerability: P1 53%, P2 53%, P3 58%, P4 61% (P4 'instruction sandwich' most effective)
Distribution of models by number of prompts they failed: 0 -> 14% (5/36 fully resistant), 1 -> 25%, 2 -> 11%, 3 -> 22%, 4 -> 28% (10/36 fully vulnerable)
Pearson correlation of prompt-success vectors: P1-P2 0.71 (strongest), P1-P3 0.48, P1-P4 0.49, P2-P3 0.42, P3-P4 0.41, P2-P4 0.28 (weakest)
Random-forest feature importance: parameters 0.75 vs model type 0.25
SHAP mean values: parameters 0.147 vs model type 0.075
Logistic regression: 86% accuracy; coefficients parameters -0.34 and model type -0.02 (both negative -> larger/typed models slightly less likely vulnerable)
PCA + K-means: 3 clusters, silhouette score 0.35; Cluster 1 (largest parameters) = lowest vulnerability, Cluster 2 (smallest parameters) = highest vulnerability, Cluster 0 = moderate
baselines_compared: No methodological baseline or competing attack/defense method is benchmarked-the study is descriptive, not comparative

Findings are qualitatively compared (in Background/Discussion) to prior related work, which are NOT this paper's results: Yu et al. 2024 (97.2% system-prompt extraction, 100% file leakage on custom GPTs), Yang et al. 2024 TAPI (98.3% on code LLMs), Zhang et al. 2024 goal-hijacking injection on robots (100% without defenses), Clusmann et al. 2024 (297 attacks on medical VLMs), Toyer et al. 2023 Tensor Trust (126k attacks/46k defenses), Zhang et al. 2023 TrojanSQL (99% fine-tuned / 89% prompt-based), Huang et al. 2024 (17.83% cross-prompt improvement)
